participants.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official study closely on setting, eligibility criteria, required telephone number, randomised sample size, and baseline demographics, with only minor paraphrasing and a noted portal error affecting exclusions.
participants.extracted: Adult employees aged over 18 years from three large UK employers in the transport, health, and communications sectors who volunteered through workplace promotion and online screening; eligible participants had depressive symptoms on the PHQ-9 (scoring 2 or more on five of nine items, including item 1 or 2) plus at least one work/home/social difficulty, and were required to provide a telephone number for weekly follow-up. A total of 9305 website visits led to 1111 screening completions and 637 randomized participants (319 control, 318 MoodGYM). The sample was approximately half male and half female, with a mean age of about 42 years, and included employees across a range of occupational grades. Individuals with brain injury, stroke, or current CBT/bipolar treatment were intended to be excluded, although some were inadvertently retained because of a portal error.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description omits the weekly telephone-call support and control-condition details, and it slightly differs on delivery and structure compared with the official intervention description.
interventions.extracted: The intervention was MoodGYM, a freely available interactive computerized cognitive behavioural therapy (cCBT) programme developed at the Australian National University. It consists of five approximately 1-hour modules that participants can work through at their own pace, typically one module per week over a 5-week period. Participants in the intervention arm accessed the programme online through a study portal and were directed to complete the MoodGYM modules as part of the trial.

Duration, frequency, length: Five weeks, weekly, 1 h‐long modules
Communication means: Internet, assisted by weekly telephone calls
Providers: not reported

outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include multiple measures and time points beyond the official outcomes, and the sickness absence wording does not match the official “number of sick leave days in the past six months” exactly.
outcomes.extracted: Primary outcome: subjective work-related performance measured with the Work and Social Adjustment Scale (WSAS; range 0-40, higher scores indicate more disability) at baseline, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks. Secondary outcomes: depression measured with the Patient Health Questionnaire-9 (PHQ-9; range 0-27, higher scores indicate more depressive symptoms) at baseline, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks; psychological distress measured with the Clinical Outcomes in Routine Evaluation-10 (CORE10; range 0-40, higher scores indicate more problems and distress) at baseline, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks; generalized anxiety measured with the Generalized Anxiety Disorder scale (GAD; range 0-21, higher scores indicate greater anxiety) at baseline, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks; health-related quality of life measured with the EuroQol-5D (EQ-5D; five domains: mobility, self-care, usual activities, pain/discomfort, and anxiety/depression) at baseline and follow-up for economic evaluation; self-assessed absence from work/sick leave recorded at baseline and during follow-up; acceptability of the online process assessed during the trial; and service use measured with the Client Service Receipt Inventory (CSRI) at baseline and follow-up.
